title: HardInterventionOn
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. The intervened CDMG w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: \[ G_{\doit(W)}:=(J_{\doit(W)},V_{\doit(W)},E_{\doit(W)},L_{\doit(W)}),\]

where:

  1. $J_{\doit(W)}:= J \cup W$,
  2. $V_{\doit(W)}:= V \sm W$,
  3. $E_{\doit(W)}:= E \sm \lC v \tuh w \,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$,
  4. $L_{\doit(W)}:= L \sm \lC v \huh w\,|\, v \in G, w \in W \rC$,

where we turn all nodes from $W$ into input nodes and remove all edges into nodes from $W$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let $W \subseteq J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes; we do not require $W \cap J = \emptyset$, i.e.\ $W$ is permitted to overlap the input-node set $J$.

The intervened CDMG w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG \[ G_{\doit(W)} := (J_{\doit(W)},\, V_{\doit(W)},\, E_{\doit(W)},\, L_{\doit(W)}), \]

whose four components are defined as follows.

  1. Input nodes: \[ J_{\doit(W)} := J \cup W. \]
  2. Output nodes: \[ V_{\doit(W)} := V \sm W. \]
  3. Directed edges: \[ E_{\doit(W)} := E \sm \lC (v_1, v_2) \in E \,|\, v_2 \in W \rC. \] Equivalently, rendered through def not-cdmg: every directed edge $v_1 \tuh v_2$ in $G$ whose head $v_2$ lies in $W$ is removed; every other directed edge of $G$ is retained.
  4. Bidirected edges: \[ L_{\doit(W)} := L \sm \lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \,|\, v_2 \in W \rC. \] Equivalently, rendered through def not-cdmg: every ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ (equivalently $v_1 \huh v_2$ in $G$) whose second component $v_2$ lies in $W$ is removed; pairs whose second component lies in $V \sm W$ are retained.

Intuitively, we turn all nodes from $W$ into input nodes and remove all edges into nodes from $W$.

Remark on item iv.\ and the symmetry of $L$. Under def def-cdmg's encoding of $L$ as a symmetric set of ordered pairs in $V \times V$ (the axiom $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$), the literal one-sided formula for $L_{\doit(W)}$ at item iv.\ above does not in general preserve symmetry. Concretely, take $w \in W \cap V$ and $v \in V \sm W$; by the symmetry of $L$ both ordered pairs $(v, w)$ and $(w, v)$ lie in $L$ and together represent the same bidirected edge between $v$ and $w$. The one-sided removal set $\lC (v_1, v_2) \in L \,|\, v_2 \in W \rC$ catches $(v, w)$ (its second component is $w \in W$) but not $(w, v)$ (its second component is $v \notin W$); hence $(v, w)$ is deleted while $(w, v)$ survives, and $L_{\doit(W)}$ — read literally — need not satisfy the symmetry constraint of def def-cdmg. The LN's natural-language gloss “remove all edges into nodes from $W$” and the LN's own assertion that $G_{\doit(W)}$ is itself a CDMG both demand the symmetric reading, since a bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ is into both of its endpoints (def def-edge-relations, item ii.) and since the CDMG axioms of def def-cdmg require the bidirected-edge set to be symmetric; the formal set-builder text at item iv.\ above, however, does not itself symmetrize. The reconciliation of this tension between the literal one-sided formula and the CDMG axioms is deferred to def\_3\_10's Lean encoding (downstream of this tex specification), where the constructor of $G_{\doit(W)}$ must produce a symmetric $L_{\doit(W)}$ in order to satisfy the CDMG axioms.

Disambiguation of the LN's “$v \in G$” quantifier. The LN's quantifier “$v \in G$” is shorthand for $v \in J \cup V$ (def not-cdmg, item 1); $G$ is a 4-tuple and has no direct $\in$-relation. In the rewritten removal sets at items iii.\ and iv.\ this clause has been folded into the ambient typing of the corresponding edge set ($E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ at item iii., $L \subseteq V \times V$ at item iv.), so no explicit “$v_1 \in J \cup V$” clause is needed. In particular, the directed self-loop case $(w, w) \in E$ with $w \in W$ is included in the removal set at item iii.\ (such pairs are matched by $v_2 = w \in W$); the bidirected self-loop case $(w, w) \in L$ at item iv.\ is automatically vacuous because $L$ is irreflexive (def def-cdmg).

On the case $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$. The LN hypothesis $W \subseteq J \cup V$ admits the case $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$, i.e.\ $W$ may contain nodes that are already input nodes of $G$. The four components then behave as follows: items i.\ and ii.\ are idempotent on $W \cap J$ (since $W \cap J \subseteq J$ already, $J \cup W$ does not change at those nodes, and $V \sm W$ is unaffected by $W \cap J$ because $J \cap V = \emptyset$); items iii.\ and iv.\ still fire on edges incident to $W \cap J$ at the head slot, but the typing constraints of def def-cdmg ($E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ and $L \subseteq V \times V$) force any such head to lie in $V$, so no edge of $G$ has its head in $W \cap J$ in the first place. Concretely: for $w \in W \cap J$, no directed edge $(v_1, w) \in E$ exists (since $w \notin V$), and no bidirected edge $(v_1, w) \in L$ or $(w, v_1) \in L$ exists (since $w \notin V$). Thus items iii.\ and iv.\ are also de facto no-ops at $W \cap J$, even though their set-builder clauses are written without a special case for it.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `hardInterventionOn`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the intervention construction in particular: the body uses `Finset.filter` (for the directed and bidirected edge removals), `Finset.union` (to form `G.J \cup W`), and `Finset.sdiff` (for `G.V \setminus W`) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.filter`/`union`/`sdiff` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `hardInterventionOn`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the intervention construction in particular: the body uses `Finset.filter` (for the directed and bidirected edge removals), `Finset.union` (to form `G.J \cup W`), and `Finset.sdiff` (for `G.V \setminus W`) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.filter`/`union`/`sdiff` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  J := G.J ∪ W
  V := G.V \ W
  hJV_disj := hardInterventionOn_hJV_disj G W
  E := G.E.filter (fun e => e.2 ∉ W)
  hE_subset := hardInterventionOn_hE_subset G W
  L := G.L.filter (fun s => ∀ v ∈ s, v ∉ W)
  hL_subset := hardInterventionOn_hL_subset G W
  hL_irrefl := hardInterventionOn_hL_irrefl G W
lean_blocks.explanation: This block constructs the intervened CDMG $G_{\doit(W)}$ as a fresh `CDMG Node` record via `where`-syntax. The body is pure data: the four LN-named components $(J_{\doit(W)}, V_{\doit(W)}, E_{\doit(W)}, L_{\doit(W)})$ get one-line assignments using `Finset.union`, `Finset.sdiff`, and `Finset.filter`; the four well-formedness obligations (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl`) are discharged by named `private lemma`s declared above the def, so the marker-wrapped surface reads as the LN's mathematical definition rather than a wall of `Finset.disjoint_left.mpr` / `Finset.mem_filter.mp` plumbing. The signature carries `(hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)` for LN-faithfulness; the obligations' typing constraints already supply every needed membership fact. The bidirected filter `fun s => ∀ v ∈ s, v ∉ W` walks an unordered `Sym2 Node` pair and keeps it iff *both* endpoints sit outside `W` — the symmetric reading that ensures $L_{\doit(W)}$ remains a valid `Finset (Sym2 Node)`. `hL_symm` is absent: with `L` stored as a `Sym2` quotient, symmetry holds definitionally and no separate field is needed.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: def_3_10 (main) — build `G_{do(W)}` field by field via `where`-syntax; obligations live in lifted private lemmas.
def hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    -- `hW` records the LN's `W ⊆ J ∪ V`; carried for LN-faithfulness, not consumed by the obligations.
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  -- New input set: original input nodes plus the intervened ones.
  J := G.J ∪ W
  -- New output set: original output nodes minus the intervened ones (`Finset.sdiff` via `\`).
  V := G.V \ W
  hJV_disj := hardInterventionOn_hJV_disj G W
  -- LN item iii. (one-sided): keep directed edges whose head is NOT in `W`.
  E := G.E.filter (fun e => e.2 ∉ W)
  hE_subset := hardInterventionOn_hE_subset G W
  -- LN item iv. with `Sym2`-symmetric reading: keep pairs whose BOTH endpoints lie outside `W`.
  L := G.L.filter (fun s => ∀ v ∈ s, v ∉ W)
  hL_subset := hardInterventionOn_hL_subset G W
  hL_irrefl := hardInterventionOn_hL_irrefl G W

design_choices:
**The output is a plain `def`, not a wrapping `structure` or `inductive`.** A hard intervention is a function `CDMG Node → Finset Node → … → CDMG Node`. Wrapping the result in a fresh record would force downstream consumers to re-destructure the same fields ordinary CDMGs expose.

**`hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is an explicit argument.** Subset facts are not typeclass-resolvable, so an `instance` would force every caller to discharge the membership manually. None of the four proof obligations on the output CDMG consumes `hW` — `def_3_1`'s typing axioms already discharge them — but it is carried at the signature to mirror the LN's "let $W \subseteq J \cup V$".

**Edge removals use `Finset.filter`.** The LN writes the removed sets as $E \setminus \{\ldots\}$; `Finset.filter` is the closest primitive, and `Finset.mem_filter` matches elementhood directly. `Finset.image` re-maps elements rather than takes a difference and was rejected.

**Directed-edge removal is one-sided (`e.2 ∉ W`); bidirected-edge removal is endpoint-universal (`∀ v ∈ s, v ∉ W`).** The directed channel is asymmetric: the head of $(v_1, v_2)$ is $v_2$, so the kept-condition is `e.2 ∉ W`. Item iv lives on `Finset (Sym2 Node)` — the shape from `def_3_1`'s `cdmg_typed_edges` design — where `Sym2 Node` is an *unordered* pair. The LN's literal item iv resolves to "any endpoint lies in $W$"; swap-symmetry is *definitional* on `Sym2`, so the `hard_intervention_l_symmetrized_removal` deviation that an ordered-pair `Finset (Node × Node)` encoding with an `hL_symm` axiom would force is structurally absent here. The load-bearing downstream consumer is `claim_3_22` (σ-separation symmetry), whose proof reduces to clean structural induction once bidirected steps reverse on the same `Sym2` element.

**A private `DecidablePred` instance is supplied for the `L`-filter.** Decidability of `∀ v ∈ s, v ∉ W` over `Sym2.Mem` is derived via `Sym2.recOnSubsingleton` + `Sym2.ball`. Opening `Classical` project-wide would lose kernel-computability, so the construction stays a `def`, not `noncomputable def`.

**$W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ is admitted.** Items i and ii are idempotent on $W \cap J$, and items iii and iv are de facto no-ops because `def_3_1`'s typing constraints forbid any edge from touching $J$ on the relevant side. Idempotency is not baked into the return type; downstream lemmas derive it as a corollary.

**The CDMG is built with `where`-syntax and named fields.** The CDMG carries eight fields; `hL_symm` is absent because swap-symmetry is definitional on `Sym2`. Named fields keep each obligation adjacent to the data it constrains. The four proof obligations are factored into private lemmas (`hardInterventionOn_hJV_disj`, `_hE_subset`, `_hL_subset`, `_hL_irrefl`) so the `def` body reads as pure data plus lemma references.
